title: 1.1 Big O & Complexity Analysis
summary: Recognize O(1), O(n), O(n²), O(log n). Analyze unknown functions. Write functions to match a target complexity.
sessionNotes: Solid on recognition; tripped on space vs time distinction and Python list mutation.
intro: Big O describes how runtime / memory scale with input size. Drop constants, drop smaller terms, default to worst case. Recursive calls count: each call is work + a stack frame. Space and time are independent — a fast algorithm can still be memory-hungry.

body.heading: Big O — what it measures
body.content:
Big O describes how runtime / memory scale with input size, not absolute speed.

Rules of thumb:
  - Drop constants (3n becomes O(n))
  - Drop smaller terms (n² + n becomes O(n²))
  - Default to worst case unless explicitly told otherwise

Time and space are independent. A fast algorithm can still use a lot of memory; a slow one can use very little.

body.heading: O(1) — constant time
body.content:
def get_first(lst):
    return lst[0]

# Regardless of list size, this does one lookup.
print(get_first([1, 2, 3]))
print(get_first(list(range(1000000))))

body.heading: O(n) — linear time
O(n²) — quadratic time
O(log n) — logarithmic time

exercises.title: Problem 1 — Analyze mystery_1
exercises.critique: Nested loop over n × n → O(n²) time. Only a constant number of variables → O(1) space. The trailing `total += 1` outside the inner loop doesn't change the asymptotic class — drop smaller terms.

exercises.title: Problem 2 — Analyze mystery_2
exercises.critique: Single pass through lst → O(n) time. Result grows to size n → O(n) extra space. This is the canonical "map" pattern.

exercises.title: Problem 3 — Analyze mystery_3
exercises.critique: Halving each step → O(log n) time, O(1) space. Same shape as binary search. Note: `//` floors toward -∞, not toward zero — a recurring gotcha.

exercises.title: Problem 4 — Analyze mystery_4 (recursive)
exercises.critique: Recursion of depth n/2, each call slices the list (O(n) copy). Total time: O(n²) due to slicing. Space: O(n) — the slice copies and the call stack. This is a palindrome check; slicing makes it more expensive than it looks. Lesson: `lst[1:-1]` is a COPY, not a view.

exercises.title: Challenge 1 — Write O(n) time, O(n) space
exercises.prompt: Write a function that has O(n) time and O(n) space.
exercises.critique: Correct. Single pass copy. O(n) time (one loop), O(n) space (new list of size n). This is essentially what `lst.copy()` does.

exercises.title: Challenge 2 — Write O(n²) time, O(1) space
exercises.prompt: Write a function that has O(n²) time and O(1) space.
exercises.critique: Correct shape — nested loops give O(n²). Print to stdout is side-effect only, no growing data structure → O(1) space.

exercises.title: Challenge 3 — Write O(1) time, O(1) space
exercises.prompt: Write a function that has O(1) time and O(1) space.
exercises.critique: Correct. Single operation regardless of n. Constant time, constant space.

reviewItemsAdded: Recursive calls count as time complexity
Never modify a list while iterating over it
